title: Kotlin Mutable vs Immutable Collections
content:
### Mutable vs Immutable Collections

Kotlin makes a clear distinction between read-only (immutable) and mutable collections. This is a key design decision for safety and predictability.

---

### Collection Interfaces

```
Iterable
  |-- Collection (read-only)
  |     |-- List
  |     |-- Set
  |     |-- Map (separate hierarchy)
  |
  |-- MutableIterable
        |-- MutableCollection (read-write)
              |-- MutableList
              |-- MutableSet
              |-- MutableMap (separate hierarchy)
```

---

### Read-Only vs Mutable

| Feature | Read-Only (List, Set, Map) | Mutable (MutableList, etc.) |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Add/Remove | Not supported | Supported |
| Thread safety | Safer (cannot be modified) | Not thread-safe by default |
| Underlying | May be mutable or immutable | Always mutable |

---

### Creating Collections

```kotlin
// Immutable references
val immutableList = listOf(1, 2, 3)
val immutableSet = setOf(1, 2, 3)
val immutableMap = mapOf("a" to 1)

// Mutable
val mutableList = mutableListOf(1, 2, 3)
val mutableSet = mutableSetOf(1, 2, 3)
val mutableMap = mutableMapOf("a" to 1)
```

---

### Converting Between Types

```kotlin
val mutable = mutableListOf(1, 2, 3)
val readOnly: List<Int> = mutable  // Upcast (view)

val copy = mutable.toList()  // Deep copy (truly immutable)
val backToMutable = copy.toMutableList()
```

---

### Read-Only is Not Necessarily Immutable

A read-only reference does not guarantee the underlying collection is immutable.

```kotlin
val mutable = mutableListOf(1, 2, 3)
val readOnly: List<Int> = mutable  // Read-only view

mutable.add(4)  // Modifies underlying list
println(readOnly)  // [1, 2, 3, 4]  // readOnly "changed"
```

---

### Best Practices

| Situation | Recommendation |
|-----------|---------------|
| Function parameter | Prefer read-only (`List`, `Set`, `Map`) |
| Local variable | Use `listOf()` unless modification needed |
| Class property | Use read-only, expose mutable internally |
| Return type | Return read-only interface to hide mutability |

```kotlin
class Repository {
    private val _items = mutableListOf<String>()
    val items: List<String> get() = _items.toList()  // Defensive copy
}
```

> **Key Point:** Favor read-only interfaces in public APIs. Use `toList()`, `toSet()`, `toMap()` for defensive copies. Returns read-only views preserve the ability to change the API later.

quiz.question: Is a read-only collection reference guaranteed to be immutable?
options: No, the underlying collection may be mutable

quiz.question: How do you create a defensive copy of a list?
